Re: R€quest for Fornal Advice
Dear General counsel:
I have been authorized by Lonpoc city Mayor J.D. snith and
Councilrnember Kart G. Braun to request your fornal written advice
in accordance with Government Code Section 83114 regarding the
following conflict of interest question.
BACXGROI'ND AND OVERVIET
on September 3, 1991, staff was directed by a councilmenber to
propoie an arnendment to Lompoc city code section 16-23 (a copy of
wniLn is attached) in order to renedy the regressive rate structure
of the business tax for rental units. This direction to staff sras
made during the hearing on the appeal by Mr. George Johnson
concerning the anount of his tax liability 
under Section L6-23.
Receiving an adverse ruting from the City councilr Y.. Johnson has
subsequeitly filed a lawsuit challenging that decision in Superior
Court] In addition, lilr. Johnson has fited a complaint with the
Fair PoIiticaI Practices Comrnissionr s Enforcernent Division against
Mayor J.D. Snith and Councilnember Karl Braun (FPPC No. 91-414).
The gist of the cornplaint was that Mayor Snith and Councilnenber
Braun each ouned rental units and therefore were subject to the tax
due under Section L6-23, but failed to disqualify thenselves from
voting on tlr. Johnsonrs appeal. The commission deternined that the
allegations srere unfounded and closed the file 
without formal
enforcement action.
The present request differs from FPPC No. 9:-.-4L4 in that the
proposed City Council action involves a vote on an anendment to
Lompoc City Code Section 16-23 with respect to altering the
regressive rate structure of the rentat unit business tax to
provide for a nore equitable rate structure. 
Whereas the
underlying facts in FPPC No. 9L-4L4 involved the applicability of
Section 16-23 to one individual taxpayer, the proposed City Council
action which is the subject of this request has the potential to
affect the rental unit business tax fiability 
for every owner of
three or more rental units within the City of Lompoc, including
Mayor smith and Councilman Braun.

The concern of Mayor Snith and Councilrnan Braun is whether they nay
vote on or participate in the consideratj.on of amending section 16-
23, or whether they have a conflict of interest requiring then to
abstain from voting or participating. 
As Lonpoc iJ a general Iaw
city, the disqualification of Mayor Snith and Councj-lm;mber Braun
would not prevent a quorum frorn voting on the anendrnent of Section
L6-23 .
Property Orled bv Uayo! Snith
As revealed by the attached Form 721, Mayor Snith owns one rental
property in which his financial interest exceeds gl0,OOO 1ocated
aL 237 North D Street in Lornpoc, consisting of three units. 
Under
Lompoc City Code Section L6-23, rental properties of less than
three units are exenpt fron any tax. I{ayor Smithrs tax liability
under sectj.on 16-23 is g25.oo per year.
Property Orned by Councilnenber Braun
As revealed by the attached r.orm 72L, Councilman Braun owns rental
properties in which his financial interest exceeds SlO,OOO located
at 230 North C Street and 324-328-332 North L Street in Lornpoc,
consisting of a total of L2-1,4 units. 
Councilrnember Braunrs tax
liability 
under Section 16-23 is g5O.oo per year.
ANEIJYS I8
rn order to set forth our understanding of the appricable statutes
and regulations, we have taken the liUerty -of providing the
following analysis, which we hope is of assistlnce t6 your office.
covernment code section 87100 prohibits public officials 
fron
In?\1n9, participating 
in rnaking, or atternpting to use their
officiar position to infruence, a governnentil decision in which
they have a financial interest. 
Section 87103 provides that an
officiar has a financial- interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision wirr have a material f inancia-I
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official 
or on any rear property which thL officiar has an
interest of s1,000 or more. nEgulation s 18703 provides that a
materiar financiar, effect of I 
governmentar decision on an
officialrs 
interests is 
d istinguisliabre frorn its effect on the
public generatly unless the deaision will affect tne ofticials
interest in substantially the same manner as it wirl .if""t 
.rr
menbers of the public or a significant segment of the p"lf :.".


General counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 9, L99L
Page 4
Based on Ferraro and Letter I-91-l-l-3, we believe that your office
should determine that inasnuch as Mayor Srnith owns no more than
three rental units within the City, Mayor Snith is not disqualified
from voting on or participating 
in the consideration of the
proposed anendment to Section 16-23 under the public Aenerally
exception.
The remaining question is whether Councilmember Braun is
disquatified. 
As noted above, under EcIIaIg the public Aenerally
exception is inappticable to orlrners of four or more units.
Inasmuch as Councilmember Braun ohrns 12-14 units, it would appear
that the public generally exception cannot apply to his situation.
In footnote 3 in Ferraro, however, the Commission recognized the
public Aenerally exception may apply if Landfords are a predorninant
rrtrade, industry or profession". 
The conmission proceeded to
determine that in Los Angeles, Iandlords were not a predominant
trade, industry or profession. 
While the percentage of rental
units in the city of Los Angeles \.ras not apparent from the Ferraro
decisj.on, it is submitted that the percentage of rental units in
the City of Lornpcc rnay indeed by higher than in the City of Los
Angeles. According l:o the City of Lompoc's Planning Department,
who base their information on the l-990 census, 48.L4 percent of the
City's total housing stock is occupied by renters (6,O2O units),
while only 51.86 percent of the City's total housing stock is
owner-occupied (6,484 unit-s) . 
Based on these statistics, 
we
encourage your office to rnake an independent deterrnination of
lrhether landlords are a predominant trade, industry or profession
in the City of Lompoc.
Assurning, however, that the trade, industry or profession doctrine
does not apply, Councilmember Braun may avoid disqual ification only
if it can be deterrnined that his participation will- not have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his interest.
Under Regulation S 1A702.1(a) (3) (C), a decisj,on has a material
effect if it involves the irnposition, repeal or modification of any
taxes or fees assessed or inposed on real property in which the
official 
has a direct or indirect interest of sf,ooo or more. If
this. rule is applicable without admitting of any excepti,ons, it
might appear that Councilrnember Braunis partlcipation 
would
necessarily have a material financial effect on his -interest. rn
Letter I-91-113, however, the Cornrnission applied a different
materiality 
standard, Regulation S 19702 .i(Sl . 
Under that
standard, councilnember Braunrs decision would n-ot have a rnaterial
financial effect on his interest as the decision ,""ra n"i-"rrect
his gross revenues or the varue of his assets or liabilities 
by
$10,000 or more and would not result in his i";.;i";;;-.iii-t:-""ur


General counsel
Fair Politica1 Practices Conmission
December 9, L99l
Page 3
In John Ferraro, councilmember. Los Anqeles 4 FPPC 62 (No. 78-009,
Nov. 7, 1978), the question lras whether a councilnember who owns
a sinqle-fanily rental property rnay vote on or participate in the
consideration of a proposed rent control ordinance. The Cornmission
concluded that the interests of owners of three or fewer rental
units will not be affected by rent control. decisions in a manner
distinguishable fron the effect upon a significant segnent of the
public generally, and therefore such ol^rners are not disqualified.
The commission reasoned that owners of three or fewer rental units
could not be considered part of the rental property industry. 
As
the proposed rent control ordinance would have affected all owners
of three or fewer rental units in nuch the same manner, the public
generally exception applied. The conmission also stated:
our decision in this matter does not mean that an owner
of four or more rental units will 
autonatically be
disqualified 
from participating 
in 
rent 
control
decisions. 
Although the owner of four or nore units
cannot avoid disqualification through use of the pubJ.ic
generally provision of Section a7lo3, he may stilI
participate 
in 
rent 
control 
decisions if 
the
circumstances indicate that it is not foreseeabl-e that
the decisions wilI 
have a material effect on his
financial interests. 
white we beli,eve that in nost
instances it wiLl be foreseeable that rent control
decisions wilt have a rnaterial effect upon the financial
interest of the oe/ners of four or more rental units, it
is not impossible to inagine situations where a proposed
rent control ordinance lrou1d not materially affect those
interests .
Id. at 7. 
Because the public generally exception governed the
outcome of the decj-sion, the Cornmission did not find it necessary
to address guestions of foreseeability and materiality.
In an inforrnal assistance letter (I-91-113, June L7, ).9g:-) issued
by your office, the question was whether a city councilrnember lrho
owns one rental unit nay participate i,n a governmental decision
involving a business ricenLe fee lxemption for owners of a smatt
nunber of rental units in a jurisdiction where 50 percent of the
residentiar- properties incrude at reast one rentil 
unit. 
The
Commj.ssion concluded that the 'rpubJ,ic generally'r excepti;; ;pplied
to officiars 
who own one rentil unif because the effect of the
decision on their rear property rentar business i;ter-e;;o;1d 
be
similar to the effect oi trrat aLcision "n " sig.,rii"u"J!";!"t 
"t
the pubJ.ic.


ceneral counsel
Pair Political Practices Commission
Decenber 9, 1991
Page 5
expenses or a reduction or elinination of existing expenses of
$2,500 or nore. Because of the uncertainty as to which standard
is appticable. ctarificalion as to the approprj.ate standard for
deternining materiality is requested.
CONCIJUgION
Based on the foregoing analysis of the applicable statutes,
regulations, and Commission opinions, it appears that I'layor Snith
rnay vote on and participate in the consideration of amending Lompoc
City Code Section 16-23 because he ovns no nore than three rental
units within the City. Councilhember Braun also rrill be able to
vote and participate if the rnateriality standards of Regulation S
78702.2(ql are applicable as suggested by Letter I-91-113, but nay
be disqual-ified if the standards of Regulation S 18702.1(a) (3) (C)
are applicable instead. We recognize that our conclusions are
tentative in nature and therefore request fornal vritten advice.
I trust that the foregoing analysis lrill be of sone assistance.
If additional specificity is requj.red from this office, please do
not hesitate to contact ne directly at any time.
Very truly yours,
z4
John Eastnan
Deputy City Attorney
c: 
Uayor J, D, Smith
Councilmernber Karl Braun
David Hirsch, City Attorney
llaureen Bosking, City Clerk

sections.question: The concern of Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun is whether they may vote on or participate in the consideration of amending section 16-23, or whether they have a conflict of interest requiring them to abstain from voting or participating.
sections.question_synthetic: May Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun, who own rental properties subject to Lompoc City Code Section 16-23's business tax, vote on and participate in an amendment to that tax code that would alter the rate structure and potentially affect all rental property owners in the city?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from City Attorney to FPPC requesting formal advice. The letter includes extensive analysis of applicable law and prior FPPC decisions (Ferraro, Letter I-91-113) but this is the city's own analysis, not FPPC's response. No formal FPPC conclusion section present. Document quality is poor due to OCR scanning artifacts but content is substantially readable.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The concern of Mayor Smith and Councilman Braun is whether they may vote on or participate in the consideration of amending section 16-23, or whether they have a conflict of interest requiring them to abstain from voting or participating.

embedding.summary: The City of Lompoc requests formal FPPC advice on whether Mayor Smith (3 rental units) and Councilman Braun (12-14 rental units) have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prevent them from voting on a proposed amendment to the city's business tax code for rental units.
